behaviors.description: The conversation history passed to the LLM is trimmed to the most recent 10 messages when the full history exceeds the context window.
behaviors.justification: `MOCK-MISS`: this behavior is about cross-component handoff (`history passed to the LLM`), not only the trim function itself. Unit tests can prove trimming logic, but can miss wiring bugs where orchestration sends untrimmed or wrong history. `REAL-INFRA`: no explicit real OS/network/subprocess or external round-trip requirement in the behavior text, so not System. `LLM-DEP`: correctness is about input payload shape, not model output quality, so not Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Run the message-orchestration path with a stub LLM client and a history longer than 10; assert the actual payload sent to the LLM client contains exactly the latest 10 messages in order.

behaviors.description: The generated response addresses the customer's question rather than producing a generic or off-topic reply.
behaviors.justification: `LLM-DEP`: this is directly about response relevance/quality, which depends on real model behavior; deterministic stubs would hide this failure mode. `MULTI-STEP`: behavior text asks for quality of a generated answer, not a multi-stage agent pipeline requirement, so Workflow is not required.
behaviors.test_description: Use real model calls on a representative prompt set and evaluate whether responses stay on-topic and answer the user’s question (with rubric or human/LLM-as-judge checks and variance-aware pass criteria).

behaviors.description: The tone re-check correctly identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers regeneration.
behaviors.justification: `LLM-DEP`: correctness includes tone judgment (`identifies ... too formal`), which is model-dependent semantic classification; stubs can always pass and miss real misclassification. `MULTI-STEP`: although regeneration is triggered, the key correctness hinge is the tone-classification decision; behavior text does not require validating a full multi-agent pipeline quality outcome, so Agentic is sufficient and lower than Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Feed realistic too-formal candidate responses for casual-tier cases through the real tone-check path and verify the system flags them and executes regeneration; include non-formal controls to check it does not over-trigger.

behaviors.description: The full exchange (user message, generated response, any regenerations) is persisted to the database with correct timestamps.
behaviors.justification: `SYSTEM TRIAD #3`: behavior explicitly requires proving an externally visible side effect via round-trip persistence (`persisted to the database with correct timestamps`). Unit/integration-only mocks can hide serialization/timestamp/transaction boundary issues. `LLM-DEP`: not about model output quality, so no Agentic requirement.
behaviors.test_description: Execute a real request through the running service, then read back persisted rows from the real DB path used by the service and assert all exchange entries (including regeneration attempts) and timestamp fields are present and correctly ordered.

behaviors.description: When the tone check triggers regeneration, the second response replaces the first — the customer never sees the rejected draft.
behaviors.justification: This is an externally visible contract (`customer never sees`) and requires round-trip observation at the delivery boundary, which matches `SYSTEM TRIAD #3`. Integration-level internal assertions can miss real response-surface leaks (API/UI/transport serialization). `REJECTION-GATE`: Agentic considered but rejected because the behavior text is about exposure/replacement mechanics, not quality of model decisions.
behaviors.test_description: Force a regeneration scenario, call the real customer-facing interface, and assert the returned/visible response is only the regenerated one while the rejected draft is absent from the externally observable payload/history view.
